coredata.dc:title: Platinum/nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles synthesized in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots aqueous solution for methanol electro-oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Aofeng
Li, Xingwei
He, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Platinum-based catalysts are a key to develop direct methanol fuel cells, which are usually dispersed on carbon supports to enhance catalytic activity, stability and utilization. Here, platinum/nitrogen-doped carbon (Pt/N-C) nanoparticles are synthesized in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) aqueous solution, and catalytic performance of Pt/N-C catalysts is researched for methanol electro-oxidation. Compared with Pt catalysts supported on commercial carbon black Vulcan XC-72 (Pt/XC-72), the peak current densities of Pt/N-C catalysts are 0.59mAcm−2 Pt and 385.2Ag−1 Pt in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4, while those of Pt/XC-72 catalysts are 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 64.8Ag−1 Pt. After polarizing 7200s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the catalytic activities of Pt/N-C are 0.14mAcm−2 Pt and 86.5Ag−1 Pt, and those of Pt/XC-72 are 0.02mAcm−2 Pt and 2.6Ag−1 Pt.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Platinum catalysts
Nanoparticles
Methanol electro-oxidation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electro-catalytic performance of Pt/N-C catalysts for methanol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for Pt/N-C catalysts.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs, (b) PL spectra of N-CQDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths, the illustration is photographs of N-CQDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and a 365nm UV lamp (right).
FTIR spectra of N-CQDs and rice husk-based carbon.
(a) TEM of N-CQDs, (b) HRTEM of N-CQDs, (c) TEM of Pt/N-C and (d) HRTEM of Pt/N-C, the inset is the particle size distribution of N-CQDs.
XRD patterns of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts.
XPS core level spectra of (a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) N 1s and (d) Pt4f for Pt/N-C.
CVs of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 in 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1.
CVs of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 (a) area-normalized current density and (b) mass-normalized current density.
CA curves of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 at fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), (a) area-normalized current density and (b) mass-normalized current density.
CVs of Pt/XC-72 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 after CA test (a) area-normalized current density and (b) mass-normalized current density.
CVs of Pt/N-C in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 after CA test (a) area-normalized current density and (b) mass-normalized current density.
The surface elemental analysis results of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 based on XPS.
The binding energies of Pt 4f along with their relative intensities, and the atomic compositions of varied Pt species.
Catalytic performance of several Pt-based catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation.

head.ce:title: Platinum/nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles synthesized in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots aqueous solution for methanol electro-oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials
Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Platinum-based catalysts are a key to develop direct methanol fuel cells, which are usually dispersed on carbon supports to enhance catalytic activity, stability and utilization. Here, platinum/nitrogen-doped carbon (Pt/N-C) nanoparticles are synthesized in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) aqueous solution, and catalytic performance of Pt/N-C catalysts is researched for methanol electro-oxidation. Compared with Pt catalysts supported on commercial carbon black Vulcan XC-72 (Pt/XC-72), the peak current densities of Pt/N-C catalysts are 0.59mAcm−2 Pt and 385.2Ag−1 Pt in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4, while those of Pt/XC-72 catalysts are 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 64.8Ag−1 Pt. After polarizing 7200s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the catalytic activities of Pt/N-C are 0.14mAcm−2 Pt and 86.5Ag−1 Pt, and those of Pt/XC-72 are 0.02mAcm−2 Pt and 2.6Ag−1 Pt.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have been extensively studied as an ideal energy converter that directly converts chemical energy of methanol combustion to electrical energy [1–3]. Compared to other types of fuels, as a liquid fuel at ambient conditions, methanol is easily stored, transported, handled by the existing infrastructure, and with a high solubility in aqueous electrolytes. More importantly, methanol has a high energy density [3–5]. However, there are two key obstacles preventing DMFCs from successful commercialization: a high manufacturing cost and a sluggish kinetic rate of methanol electro-oxidation. In order to improve the poor kinetics of the methanol electro-oxidation reaction, appropriate anode catalysts have been regarded as a major contributor [3]. As has been demonstrated repeatedly, platinum-based catalysts play a key role in improving the poor kinetics of the methanol electro-oxidation reaction. Unfortunately, Pt catalysts are readily poisoned by intermediate carbonaceous species (mainly CO) during the methanol electro-oxidation reaction, consequently leading to a sharp decrease in electrocatalytic activity. In addition, rising Pt price, due to the scarcity of resources, also gives a high cost. It has been confirmed that the control of distribution and morphology of Pt catalysts on supporting materials can offer effective assistance in improving catalytic activity and durability [6–9]. The excellent supporting materials should have the following characteristics: (1) a large specific surface area to achieve a high metal catalysts loading and a uniform dispersion; (2) a good stability under harsh electrochemical conditions; (3) a high electrical conductivity to promote electron transfer during the electrode reaction; (4) a strong interaction with metal catalysts to ensure the stability of the dispersed catalysts. So far, carbon materials have attracted immense and persistent attention, because they have extraordinary physical properties, abundance, processibility, environmental friendliness and relative stability in both acidic and basic media. And many studies suggest that carbon materials as catalyst supports, including carbon nanotubes [10,11], porous carbon [12,13], graphene [14] and graphene oxide [15], can improve performance of Pt-based catalysts. Currently, Vulcan XC-72 carbon black is the best commercial supporting material, but there are still some shortcomings, including low Pt loading, unsatisfactory catalytic activity and durability. Therefore, it is still a big challenge to develop new carbon supports and improve existing carbon materials.

Last few years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new member of the carbon family, have been used in many fields due to good water solubility, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, excellent conductivity and optical properties [16–20]. It is particularly worth mentioning that CQDs can be easily modified and doped by a simple chemical method due to their unique chemical structure thereby obtaining desired properties. Recently, ultra-small size of CQDs with an outstanding stability and a good electrical conductivity makes them interesting contenders as electro-catalysts supports for oxygen reduction. For example, He G. Q. et al. [21] prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from carbon fibers, and obtained Pt catalysts supported on GQDs (Pt/G). For oxygen reduction in acid electrolyte solutions, Pt/G catalysts exhibited an enhanced electrocatalytic performance with a more positive onset potential, higher specific activity as well as stability, as compared to commercial Pt/C. In another work, the silver nanoclusters supported on CQDs were synthesized through a facile and green approach with only glucose and AgNO3 as precursors and without any other protecting ligand and reducing agent [22]. Silver nanoclusters also showed remarkable electrocatalytic performance towards oxygen reduction reaction. Moreover, Kang and co-workers [23] synthesized carbon quantum dot/NiFe layered double-hydroxide composite as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER).

The structure of carbon materials can also be changed via hetero atoms doping, thus effectively controlling its intrinsic properties, including electronic property, surface structure and chemical state [24–27]. Currently, nitrogen-doped carbon materials are of concern. Nitrogen-doped carbon materials have been proposed as supports for the metal-based catalysts. Such as, Zhang L. M. et al. [28] synthesized a novel nitrogen-doped carbon material with honeycomb-like mesoporous structure via carbonization of polyaniline under template and acted as a support for Pt catalysts. Authors think that an improved performance of Pt catalysts should be attributed to N species introduced to the support and optimal proportion of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N. Some studies also have shown that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with abundant structural defects can promote charges transfer from catalysts to reactants as well as to improve the dispersal of Pt catalysts, because amounts of defects at surface and edge can generate more active sites [21,29–31]. Several methods have been used to synthesize N-doped carbon quantum dots. For instance, N. Parvin et al. [32] reported the synthesis of a highly fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots bioimaging probe. N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of agarose and ethylenediamine. In this work, mono-dispersed N-CQDs averaging 4.5nm in diameter were achieved in a quantum yield of 74.16%. Li Y. et al. [33] developed a simple electrochemical strategy for generating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) by using N-containing tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) in acetontrile to introduce N atoms into the resultant graphene quantum dots in situ. In another study, Shen J. H. et al. [34] presented a simple hydrothermal treatment of melamine and glutaraldehyde to synthesize carbon-nitrogen quantum dots (N-CQDs). The resulting carbon-nitrogen quantum dots have a uniform morphology, with a size of ca. 5nm, plentiful oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, and a high N/C atomic ratio of ca. 20.7%. Furthermore, Te-Fu Yeh et al. [35] reported N-doped graphene oxide quantum dots as photocatalysts for water-splitting under visible light illumination, which N-doped graphene oxide quantum dots were synthesized by treating graphene oxide in NH3 at 500°C, and then subjecting the NH3-treated graphene oxide to oxidation using a modified version of Hummers’ method. Natural nitrogen-containing biomass materials are usually selected as raw materials for synthesizing N-doped carbon quantum dots, such as, Zhu C. Z. et al. [36] developed a simple, low cost and green strategy toward water-soluble, N-doped fluorescent carbon nanodots by the hydrothermal treatment of soy milk. In addition, there were also fruit juice [37], animal hair [38] and plant fiber [39].

In this study, rice husk-based carbon is used as a nitrogen-containing carbon. Firstly, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are obtained by etching rice husk-based carbon using a mixture of sulfuric acid and nitric acid. Then, platinum/nitrogen-doped carbon (Pt/N-C) catalysts are prepared by a simple chemical reduction method in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) aqueous solution using H2PtCl6 as a metal precursor, and the catalytic performance of Pt/N-C catalysts is researched for methanol electro-oxidation. As compared to Pt catalysts supported by commercial carbon black Vulcan XC-72 (Pt/XC-72), the electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of Pt/N-C catalysts is 65.3m2 g−1 Pt, which is higher than that of Pt/XC-72 (15.7m2 g−1 Pt), indicating a good dispersion of Pt catalysts. In 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4, the peak current densities of Pt/N-C are 0.59mAcm−2 Pt and 385.2Ag−1, while those of Pt/XC-72 catalysts are 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 64.8Ag−1 Pt. Furthermore, Pt/N-C catalysts also exhibit a better stability than Pt/XC-72. After polarizing 7200s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the area and mass-normalized catalytic activities of Pt/N-C are 0.14mAcm−2 Pt and 86.5Ag−1 Pt, and those of Pt/XC-72 are 0.02mAcm−2 Pt and 2.6Ag−1 Pt. The area and mass-normalized catalytic activities of Pt/N-C catalysts are 7 and 33 times higher than Pt/XC-72 catalysts.

In the preparation process of N-doped carbon quantum dots, a byproduct (silicon oxides contained ∼10% carbon) is also obtained. After 700°C heat treatment for 1h, the carbon content of silicon oxides is reduced to 3–4%. The preparation process for Pt/N-C catalysts is depicted in Scheme 1 .

Rice husk-based carbon was supplied by Jiangnan University, which the content of nitrogen was 7–8%. H2PtCl6 and NaBH4 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Vulcan XC-72 powder was supplied by Cabot and Akzonobel. Nafion solution was purchased from Dupont. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received.

First, rice husk-based carbon was grinded into powder, and then 1.0g powder was dispersed in mixed acid solution contained 30mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) and 10mL of concentrated nitric acid (65%). This black suspension was refluxed for 6h at 130°C. After cooling down to room temperature, it was diluted to 400mL with de-ionized water, and pH was adjusted to 7 with NaOH in an ice-bath. The product solution was obtained by centrifugation, and was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) for 3 days. The dialyzed N-CQDs solution was frozen for 3h in the lyophilizer, with the cold trap temperature of −65°C, then dried for 24h at 0°C to get N-CQDs powder. The precipitate after centrifugation was black powder. Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS), which was performed on QUANTAX 400-30 spectrometer, showed that the precipitate was silicon oxide containing carbon∼10%. After 700°C heat treatment for 1h, the carbon content of silicon oxides was reduced to 3–4%, and the black powder turned grey powder. Silicon oxides were of a particle diameter of less than 50nm (probed by the Hitachi S-4800 field-emission scanning electron microscopy). (See Fig. S1 and Scheme 1)

Pt/N-C catalysts were synthesized by a chemical reduction method. First, 30mg N-CQDs were dispersed into 10mL de-ionized water by ultrasonic and stirring for 10min, then adding 1mL 0.0386M H2PtCl6 under stirring. After that, 40mg NaBH4 was slowly added to above mixed solution, with stirring for 3h at 55°C, and the mixed solution became the black suspension. Finally, the black solid product was washed several times with de-ionized water and ethanol, respectively, and was dried under a vacuum condition of 60°C. Pt/XC-72 catalysts were synthesized by the same processes for comparison.

The UV-visible absorption spectra (UV/Vis) were carried out on 756S UV–vis spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–1000nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained with Fluorolog-3-P Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded by using a Nicolet Magna-IR 550 spectrometer. The morphology of catalysts was probed by a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) under bright-field image mode. Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a D/max2550VB/PC (Rigaku Co., Japan), using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056Å), at operating voltage of 40kV and current of 100mA, with 2 theta range of 5–90° and a scan rate of 1.2°min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using Al Ka X-ray radiation as the X-ray source. The binding energy was calibrated using a C 1s photoelectron peak at 284.6eV as a reference.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out on Princeton 2273 electrochemical working station. The glassy carbon electrode was used as the counter electrode. The saturated mercury sulfate electrode (MSE, Hg/Hg2SO4) was used as a reference electrode. Working electrode was prepared as follows: 2.0mg Pt/N-C catalysts were dispersed in 1mL de-ionized water by ultrasonic 20min. After that, 10μL of above suspension was dropped on a pre-cleaned glassy carbon electrode (3mm of diameter). Last, 5μL of Nafion ® solution (5wt. %) was dropped. The catalytic performance for methanol electro-oxidation was studied by cyclic voltammograms (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). CV curves were recorded in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1. CA curves were obtained by polarization at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4) for 7200s. High-purity nitrogen was purged before all measurements.

To confirm that as-prepared N-CQDs is quantum dots, UV/Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-CQDs are recorded. Fig. 1 a gives UV/Vis spectrum of N-CQDs. It can be found that there are three characteristic absorption peaks at around 230, 295 and 375nm. The peak at 230nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC sp2 domains, while the other two peaks at 295 and 375nm are ascribed to the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states [40–42]. Fig. 1b is PL spectra of N-CQDs aqueous solution with different excitation wavelengths, and photographs of N-CQDs aqueous solution under a 365nm UV lamp and sunlight are shown in the illustration. N-CQDs solution has a cyan fluorescence under a 365nm UV light, which is according with PL spectra. In Fig. 1b, the emission peak of N-CQDs aqueous solution shifts from 425 to 558nm with an increased excitation wavelength from 300 to 500nm. Such excitation-dependent PL behavior is associated with the sizes and unique surface states of N-CQDs [43,44]. When N-CQDs aqueous solution is excited at 420nm, a strongest emission peak is located at 515nm. PL behavior suggests that N-CQDs are a promising energy converter. Perhaps, PL behavior of N-CQDs is also advantageous for the reduction of platinum ions. FTIR shows that N-CQDs possess abundant oxygen-containing groups, and the presence of nitrogen is also confirmed [17,37], as shown in Fig. 2 .

Fig. 3 gives TEM of Pt/N-C, and compared with that of N-CQDs. From Fig. 3a, it can be found that the size distribution of N-CQDs particles is 3∼5nm. In Fig. 3b, a crystal lattice spacing of 0.341nm is found, which agrees with the (002) face of graphite carbon [24,45]. Fig. 3c shows that Pt/N-C catalysts are agglomerated nanoparticles. However, a lattice spacing of 0.349nm is also found in HRTEM image of Pt/N-C (Fig. 3d), corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphite [24,45]. Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.227 and 0.197nm is ascribed to Pt (111) and Pt (200) crystallographic plane in HRTEM image of Pt/N-C [46]. For the typically carbon supports (carbon black, carbon nanotubes and graphenes), Pt nanoclusters are usually distributed on these supports. However, it is difficult to distinguish Pt nanoclusters and N-CQDs, even in HRTEM of Pt/N-C (Fig. 3d). Compared with typically carbon supports, N-CQDs possess a good water-soluble due to abundant oxygen-containing groups. When N-CQDs and H2PtCl6 aqueous solution are mixed, a stable liquid system can be formed. After adding a reducing agent, the resulting Pt/N-C catalysts are precipitated from the solution. The ultra-small size of N-CQDs provides a large specific surface area to facilitate a uniform dispersion of a large number of Pt atoms, and N-CQDs with amounts of defects at surface and edge can also generate more active sites to ensure the stability of a high loading of Pt catalysts. Based on TEM images of Pt/N-C and N-CQDs, Pt should be deposited on the surface of N-CQDs, generating Pt/N-C nanoparticles. Table 1 gives the surface elemental analysis results based on XPS for Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72. This result also shows that Pt/N-C catalysts have been obtained, and Pt content is up to 60.7wt. % in Pt/N-C, and Pt content is only 24.4wt. % in Pt/XC-72 catalysts.

Fig. 4 gives the XRD patterns of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts. In the XRD patterns of as-prepared catalysts, the diffraction peaks at about 39.5°, 46.3°, 67.6° and 81.4° are ascribed to Pt (111), Pt (200), Pt (220) and Pt (311) crystallographic planes of Pt metal, respectively, indicating that Pt catalysts have the face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure [47,48], which is well accorded with HRTEM image. Additionally, Pt/XC-72 catalysts show an obvious characteristic diffraction of the graphite (002) planes at around 24.9°, but it shifts to a lower degree (∼22.5°) in Pt/N-C catalysts due to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing groups in N-CQDs [49]. Combined with the results of XRD and TEM, it can be concluded that Pt/N-C catalysts have been prepared successfully.

Fig. 5 gives the binding energies of C1s, O1s, N1s, Pt4f peak spectra for Pt/N-C. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be reasonably deconvoluted into four well resolved peaks corresponding to the following carbon functional groups: CC (284.6eV), CN (285.6eV), CO (286.5eV) and CO/CN (288.7eV) [50,51]. As shown in the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, the peaks at 531.0, 531.9 and 532.9eV correspond to CO, COC and OCO, respectively [52,53]. In the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 5c), the peaks at 398.4, 399.8 and 400.7eV are ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrroic N and quaternary N, respectively [30,50]. Because the catalytic activity of Pt catalysts depends on the chemical state of Pt, Pt 4f core level spectra of Pt/N-C are also shown in Fig. 5, and that of Pt/XC-72 catalysts are used for comparison (See Fig. S2). Three valences of Pt can be identified and further analyzed based on doublet binding energy of Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2. Two pairs of peaks from the spin-orbital splitting of Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 regions are due to Pt (II) valence state and Pt (IV) valence state [54]. According to the area of each peak in Pt 4f spectra, the atomic compositions of varied Pt species are calculated. The binding energies of Pt 4f along with their relative intensities are provided in Table 2 .

Table 2 shows that the atomic ratio of Pt (0)/Pt (II)/Pt (IV) is ∼59/25/16 for Pt/N-C catalysts, while that of Pt/XC-72 is ∼42/34/24. Compared with Pt/XC-72, Pt/N-C catalysts have a high content of Pt (0) and a low content of Pt (IV), indicating that Pt/N-C catalysts have higher durability than Pt/XC-72 due to the corrosion resistance of Pt (0) [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-catalytic performance of Pt/N-C catalysts for methanol
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic activity of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts is contrasted for methanol electro-oxidation. In many electro-catalytic studies, the current densities can be normalized by the electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of Pt catalysts. And hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks are usually used to evaluate EASA of Pt. Therefore, CV curves of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts are recorded in 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50mVs−1. As shown in Fig. 6 , the characteristic hydrogen adsorption/desorption signals from (110) and (100) planes of polycrystalline Pt are observed. EASA of Pt can be calculated with coulombic charges accumulated during hydrogen adsorption/desorption from the following equation [55,56]:
E A S A =
Q H
[
Pt
] × 0.21
where QH (mC) is the electrical charge of hydrogen adsorption integrated from CV curves, assuming monolayer hydrogen atom coverage and the hydrogen adsorption electrical charge being 0.21mCcm−2 smooth Pt surface. [Pt] is the loading of Pt on the working electrode. The calculation results show that EASA of Pt/N-C is 65.3m2 g−1 Pt, and that of Pt/XC-72 is 15.7m2 g−1 Pt. Obviously, EASA of Pt/N-C catalysts is about 4 times than that of Pt/XC-72, which should be attributed to a uniform distribution of Pt.

Fig. 7 displays CV curves of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4. The catalytic activity is evaluated as the area and mass-normalized current densities. In CV curves of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts, two anodic peaks are observed during the methanol electro-oxidation, corresponding to two different states of Pt surface. It is widely accepted that Pt occurs oxidation in the forward scan, followed by methanol oxidation that takes place on the oxidized surface of Pt catalysts. It is interesting to note that following Pt reduction during the downward voltage sweep, the activity of Pt/N-C catalysts is comparable to or even higher than that of Pt/N-C in the forward scan. It is tempting to speculate that the comparable activity is due to a high rate of methanol oxidation on almost oxide-free surface of Pt catalysts that, following reduction, is initially covered with hydroxyls rather than chemisorbed CO [57,58]. In the forward scan, the peak current densities of Pt/N-C are 0.59mAcm−2 Pt and 385.2Ag−1 Pt, but those of Pt/XC-72 are 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 64.8Ag−1 Pt. Clearly, Pt/N-C catalysts have a higher current density compared with Pt/XC-72, regardless of mass specific activity or area specific activity. Especially, the mass specific activity of Pt/N-C catalysts is about 6 times more than that of Pt/XC-72, which should be attributed to highly dispersed Pt catalysts, resulting in a high utilization of Pt.

Table 3 summarizes the catalytic performance of several Pt-based catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation [12–15,59]. In Table 3, Pt/OMC represents Pt supported on meso/microporous carbons, Pt/HNPHCS is Pt supported on N-doped porous hollow carbon spheres, Pt/Graphene is in the name of Pt supported on graphene, Pt/RGO represents Pt supported on reduced graphene oxide, Pt/SiO2 is on behalf of Pt supported on silica hollow spheres, E-TEK is in the name of commercial Pt/C catalysts, and Pt/N-C is as-prepared catalysts. Compared to Pt-based catalysts reported in these literatures, Pt/N-C catalysts also show a high mass specific activity.

To further compare the activity and stability of Pt/N-C and Pt/XC-72 catalysts, CA measurements were also performed at 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4) in 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4 solution, as shown in Fig. 8 . It is clear that Pt/N-C catalysts still show a better catalytic activity than that of Pt/XC-72. After polarizing 7200s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the current densities of Pt/N-C catalysts are 0.14mAcm−2 Pt and 86.5Ag−1 Pt, but those of Pt/XC-72 are 0.02mAcm−2 Pt and 2.6Ag−1 Pt. The area and mass-normalized current densities of Pt/N-C catalysts is 7 and 33 times higher than Pt/XC-72.

Because the CA testing process is not continuous injection of methanol, the decrease of methanol concentration may also cause the decrease of current densities. Therefore, CVs of Pt/XC-72 and Pt/N-C are once again probed following the CA measurement in a fresh methanol (0.5M CH3OH+0.5M H2SO4), and results are shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10 . Fig. 9 shows that the forward anodic peak current density of Pt/XC-72 catalysts has a slight decrease after polarizing 7200s. Reducing current density may be attributed to the loss of Pt active sites with the increase of working time. Unlike Pt/XC-72, the forward anodic peak current density of Pt/N-C catalysts shows a slight increase after polarizing 7200s, implying that Pt/N-C can maintain a good catalytic activity in a long term (Fig. 10).

In summary, N-CQDs derived from rice husk-based carbon have been synthesized, and a byproduct (silicon oxides) is also obtained realizing the multilevel utilization of agricultural wastes. Following, the advanced Pt/N-C catalysts are prepared through a simple chemical method. In 0.5M CH3OH and 0.5M H2SO4, the peak current densities of Pt/N-C are 0.59mAcm−2 Pt and 385.2Ag−1 Pt, while those of Pt/XC-72 catalysts are 0.39mAcm−2 Pt and 64.8Ag−1 Pt. After polarizing 7200s at a fixed potential of 0.15V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the area and mass-normalized current densities of Pt/N-C is 7 and 33 times higher than Pt/XC-72. Therefore, carbon quantum dots (CQD), as oxygen-rich carbon materials, can be introduced to DMFCs as an important component of catalysts.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.07.119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DMFCs: from Fundamental Aspects to Technology Development
One-Dimensional Noble Metal Electrocatalysts: a Promising Structural Paradigm for Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
Mechanism and Electrocatalysis in the Direct Methanol Fuel Cell Recent Progress on Carbon-Based Support Materials for Electrocatalysts of Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
Mechanism and Electrocatalysis in the Direct Methanol Fuel Cell
Combinatorial Electrochemistry: a Highly Parallel Optical Screening Method for Discovery of Better Electrocatalysts
PtRu/Carbon Nanotube Nanocomposite Synthesized in Supercritical Fluid: a Novel Electrocatalyst for Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
Recent Advances in Catalysts for Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
